expectations.text: Correctly identifies the user as a higher rate (40%) taxpayer
expectations.evidence: Response explicitly states: 'At £72,000, your income already exceeds the basic rate band (£50,270). This means you are a **higher rate taxpayer** for CGT purposes'

expectations.text: Mentions the CGT annual exempt amount (£3,000 for 2024/25)
expectations.evidence: Response clearly states: 'For 2024/25, the CGT Annual Exempt Amount is **£3,000**'

expectations.text: Explains the mid-year CGT rate change on 30 October 2024 or different rates before/after that date
expectations.evidence: Response provides detailed explanation with table showing: '6 Apr 2024 – 29 Oct 2024: 20% (higher rate taxpayer)' and '30 Oct 2024 – 5 Apr 2025: 24% (higher rate taxpayer)'

expectations.text: Explains SIP 3-5 year holding period means partial income tax and NI liability on shares
expectations.evidence: Response explains the 3-5 year tier: 'Lower of market value at award OR market value at withdrawal — taxed as employment income' and provides example: 'At £72,000, your marginal rate is **40%** (plus NI at **2%** above the Upper Earnings Limit of £50,270). The combined income tax and NI cost on that £5,000 would be approximately £2,100.'

expectations.text: States Plan 2 student loan repayment threshold (£27,295) and rate (9%)
expectations.evidence: Response states: '**9% of income above £27,295** per year'

expectations.text: Calculates or explains the student loan repayment amount
expectations.evidence: Response calculates: 'At £72,000: Income above threshold: £72,000 − £27,295 = **£44,705**, Annual repayment due: £44,705 × 9% = **£4,023**'

Response clearly states: '**Yes.** You have a capital gain above the Annual Exempt Amount (£4,500 gain vs £3,000 exempt amount — net taxable gain of £1,500). Capital gains that result in a tax liability must be reported via Self Assessment.'

expectations.text: Includes a disclaimer that this is not professional tax advice
expectations.evidence: Response begins with: '**DISCLAIMER**: This response provides general tax information only. It is NOT professional tax advice. Tax situations vary — always verify with HMRC guidance or a qualified accountant before making decisions.'
